Glossary/Dynamic-Vs-Static-Linked.title: Dynamic Vs Static Linked
Glossary/Dynamic-Vs-Static-Linked.content:
Dynamic linking = library tidak diikutsertakan saat proses compile, melainkan hanya disimpan sebagai referensi. Library tersebut akan dicari dan di-load saat program dijalankan (runtime).
Static linking = library diikutsertakan langsung ke dalam binary saat proses compile, sehingga program tidak membutuhkan library eksternal saat dijalankan.
Contoh
Misalnya program menggunakan fungsi printf dari libc:


Dynamic linking
Binary tidak menyimpan isi printf, tetapi akan mengambilnya dari libc.so saat runtime.


Static linking
Binary sudah menyimpan printf di dalamnya sejak compile, jadi bisa langsung dijalankan tanpa butuh libc dari sistem.


When we know a binary is statically or dynamically linked, what information can we derive from it?
Answer: We can check what dependencies the binary needs, so we can provide the required libraries and run it properly.


                  
                  Explanation 
                  
                

If a binary uses Dynamic Linking, it means the program depends on external libraries (such as libc) that must be available at runtime.
We can check these dependencies using:
ldd ./binary
From this, we know what libraries are required to run the program correctly.
In contrast, if the binary uses Static Linking, all libraries are already included during compile time, so no external dependencies are needed.




                  
                  more advance 
                  
                

Beyond just running the binary, this information can also be useful for more advanced analysis, such as identifying possible exploitation paths:

ret2libc
GOT / PLT hijacking

⚠️ Note (belum paham):

ret2libc → belum paham
GOT / PLT → belum paham

(akan dipelajari dan diupdate nanti)

Glossary/Stripped-Vs-not-Stripped.title: Stripped Vs not Stripped
Glossary/Stripped-Vs-not-Stripped.content:
In GDB, we can use the following command to list functions:
info functions
Not Stripped
0x401176  win
0x40118b  vuln
0x4011b0  main

👉 Function names such as main, vuln, and win are clearly visible.
Stripped
Non-debugging symbols:
0x0000000000401000  _init
0x0000000000401030  puts@plt
0x0000000000401040  fgets@plt
...

👉 Functions like main or win are not visible anymore.
Why?
When a binary is not stripped, it contains a full symbol table, including:

User-defined functions (e.g., main, win, vuln)
Debug-related symbols (e.g., variable names, function names, and source-level information used by debuggers)

This makes it easy to identify functions and understand the program structure.
When a binary is stripped, most of the symbol information is removed, especially:

User-defined function names (main, win, etc)
Debug symbols such as:

Local/global variable names
Function names used for debugging
Source file references (e.g., main.c, line numbers)



As a result, functions like main or win are no longer visible in tools like GDB.
However, you may still see symbols like:

_init
puts@plt
fgets@plt

These remain because:

_init is part of the program’s initialization code provided by the runtime
puts@plt, fgets@plt are entries in the PLT (Procedure Linkage Table) used for dynamic linking
These are required for the program to correctly call external libraries at runtime

Note: The actual code is still present in both cases. Only the symbol information is removed.

PicoCTF-2026---Write-Up/Inex/1.-QuizPlot.title: 1. QuizPlot
PicoCTF-2026---Write-Up/Inex/1.-QuizPlot.content:
This challenge is presented in a quiz format, where we need to answer a series of questions about the given binary.
By analyzing the binary step by step, we can answer each question and eventually obtain the flag.

Answer: 64-bit


                  
                  Explanation 
                  
                

Untuk mengetahui informasi mengenai file binary, kita bisa menggunakan command file [filename].
Command ini akan menampilkan detail seperti tipe file, arsitektur (32-bit atau 64-bit), serta apakah binary tersebut menggunakan static atau dynamic linking.




Answer: Dynamic linking


                  
                  Explanation 
                  
                

for answering this question bisa juga pakai command file [filename]

Conclusion : File using Dynamic linking. See Dynamic vs Static Linking for a more detailed explanation.



Answer: Not stripped


                  
                  Explanation 
                  
                

Untuk mengecek apakah binary stripped atau tidak, kita bisa menggunakan command:
checksec [filename]  

Dari output tersebut, kita bisa melihat apakah binary masih memiliki symbol (not stripped) atau sudah dihapus (stripped).


Conclusion: The binary is not stripped. More on this in Stripped Vs not Stripped

Answer: 0x15


                  
                  Explanation 
                  
                

Ukuran buffer bisa dilihat langsung dari deklarasi buffer di dalam fungsi vuln():
char buffer[0x15];  

Dari sini kita tahu bahwa ukuran buffer adalah 0x15 bytes (21 bytes dalam desimal).



Answer: 0x90


                  
                  Explanation vuln(), terdapat :
                  
                
Di dalam fungsi 

fgets(buffer, 0x90, stdin);  
Hal ini berarti program membaca input dari stdin dengan maksimum sebanyak 0x90 bytes, lalu memasukkannya ke dalam variable buffer.



Answer: Yes


                  
                  Explanation 
                  
                

The buffer has a size of only 0x15 bytes:
char buffer[0x15];  
However, the program reads input using:
fgets(buffer, 0x90, stdin);  
This means the program can read up to 0x90 bytes, which is much larger than the buffer size.
This results in a buffer overflow, since more data can be written than the buffer can hold.
It is important to understand that fgets does not know the actual size of the buffer. It only follows the size argument given to it (0x90).
Additionally, the char buffer itself does not perform any bounds checking. In C, there is no automatic protection against writing past the allocated memory.
Because of this, the program will not throw an error and will continue writing beyond the buffer, leading to an overflow.



Answer: fgets


                  
                  Explanation 
                  
                

The function fgets can cause a buffer overflow in this case because it reads more data than the buffer can hold.
fgets(buffer, 0x90, stdin);  
Here, fgets is allowed to read up to 0x90 bytes, while the buffer size is only 0x15 bytes:
char buffer[0x15];  
Since fgets does not know the actual size of the buffer and only follows the given argument (0x90), it can write beyond the buffer boundary, leading to a buffer overflow.



Answer: win()


                  
                  Explanation 
                  
                


The program only calls vuln() from main(), and that’s it—win() is never used.



Answer: Buffer Overflow


                  
                  Explanation 
                  
                

From the previous analysis, we identified that the program uses:
fgets(buffer, 0x90, stdin);  
while the buffer size is only:
char buffer[0x15];  
This means the program reads more data than the buffer can hold.
Since fgets can write beyond the buffer boundary without any bounds checking, this vulnerability is classified as a buffer overflow.



Answer: 0x7B


                  
                  Explanation 
                  
                

The number of overflow bytes refers to how many extra bytes can be written beyond the allocated buffer size.
In this case, the program reads up to 0x90 bytes:
fgets(buffer, 0x90, stdin);  
While the buffer size is only 0x15 bytes:
char buffer[0x15];  
To calculate the overflow:
0x90 - 0x15 = 0x7B  

This means 0x7B bytes can overflow beyond the buffer and overwrite adjacent memory.



Answer: NX enabled


                  
                  Explanation 
                  
                


Based on the checksec output:
NX: NX enabled  

This means the stack is non-executable, preventing code execution directly from the stack.
Other protections:

Canary: Not found
PIE: No PIE
RELRO: Partial RELRO

Although there are also features like SHSTK and IBT, these are more advanced CPU-level protections and are not typically the main focus in basic binary exploitation.



Answer: ROP (Return-Oriented Programming)


                  
                  Explanation 
                  
                

NX (Non-Executable) prevents execution of code on the stack, meaning we cannot directly run shellcode placed in the buffer.
To bypass this, we use ROP (Return-Oriented Programming), which works by reusing existing code in the binary.
Instead of executing code from the stack, ROP chains together small instruction sequences (gadgets) that already exist in executable memory.
This is typically done by overwriting the return address to point to another function (such as win()), or chaining multiple gadgets to achieve arbitrary behavior.



Answer: 0x401176


                  
                  Explanation 
                  
                

The address of the win() function can be found using GDB:
info functions

From the output:
0x0000000000401176  win

Therefore, the address of win() is 0x401176.


Conclusion
After answering all questions correctly, we obtain the flag.

The questions can be solved one by one manually, or automated using tools like pwntools.
Below is the exploit script used:

PicoCTF-2026---Write-Up/Inex/2.-Echo-Esc.title: 2. Echo Escape 1
PicoCTF-2026---Write-Up/Inex/2.-Echo-Esc.content:
Challenge Overview
In this challenge, we are given:

A binary file: vuln
The source code: vuln.c

Our goal is to analyze the binary and identify any vulnerabilities that can be exploited to retrieve the flag.
Checksec Analysis


                  
                  Explanation 
                  
                

We start by analyzing the binary using checksec:

From this, we can infer:

No canary → no protection against buffer overflow
NX enabled → cannot execute shellcode on the stack
No PIE → binary addresses are fixed
Partial RELRO → GOT is still writable
Not stripped → function names are visible



File Analysis


                  
                  Explanation 
                  
                

Next, we check the binary type using:

This tells us:

The binary is 64-bit
It uses dynamic linking
It is not stripped, so function names are available



Source Code Analysis


                  
                  Explanation 
                  
                

Looking at the source code:

char buf[32];
read(0, buf, 128);
We can observe:

Buffer size = 32 bytes
Input read = 128 bytes

The program reads more data than the buffer can hold.


Vulnerability


                  
                  Explanation 
                  
                

This results in a buffer overflow vulnerability.
The function read() does not perform bounds checking and will write up to 128 bytes into a 32-byte buffer.
As a result, this allows overwriting adjacent memory such as:

Saved base pointer (RBP)
Return address (RIP)

This gives us control over the program execution flow.


Finding the Address of win()


                  
                  Explanation 
                  
                

First, we use GDB to find the address of the win() function:
info functions

Since the binary is not stripped, function names like win() are still visible.
From the output, we can locate:
0x401256  win

This is the address of the win() function.
Additionally, because the binary has No PIE, this address is fixed and can be used directly in our exploit.


Reading the Disassembly of main()


                  
                  Explanation 
                  
                

Before calling read(), the program prepares its arguments in registers.
On x86-64 Linux, function arguments are passed using registers in this order:

rdi → 1st argument
rsi → 2nd argument
rdx → 3rd argument
rcx → 4th argument

In the disassembly, we see:

This corresponds to:
read(0, buf, 128);
The setup works as follows:


lea rax, [rbp-0x20]
Loads the address of the local buffer into rax


mov edx, 0x80
Sets the 3rd argument to 0x80 (128), which is the number of bytes to read


mov rsi, rax
Sets the 2nd argument to the buffer address


mov edi, 0x0
Sets the 1st argument to 0, which means input is read from stdin


call read@plt
Calls read(0, buf, 128)


From lea rax, [rbp-0x20], we know the buffer starts at offset rbp-0x20, meaning it occupies 32 bytes on the stack.
Since the saved rbp is located right above it, the offset to the return address becomes:
0x20 + 0x8 = 0x28

So, 40 bytes are needed to overwrite.


Solve.py



                  
                  Explanation 
                  
                

Explanation of the payload:

b"A" * 32 fills the buffer
b"B" * 8 overwrites the saved RBP
p64(win_addr) overwrites the saved return address with the address of win()

This works because the buffer is 32 bytes long, and the saved RBP is located directly after it on the stack.
Therefore, after 40 bytes, the next value written will overwrite the return address.
Once main() finishes and returns, execution jumps to win() instead of the normal return location, which causes the program to print the flag.


Flag

PicoCTF-2026---Write-Up/Inex/3.-Echo-Esc.title: 3. Echo Escape 2
PicoCTF-2026---Write-Up/Inex/3.-Echo-Esc.content:
Challenge Overview
In this challenge, we are given:

A binary file: vuln
The source code: vuln.c

Our goal is to analyze the binary and identify any vulnerabilities that can be exploited to retrieve the flag.
Checksec Analysis


                  
                  Explanation 
                  
                


From this, we can infer:

No canary → no protection against buffer overflow
NX enabled → cannot execute shellcode on the stack
No PIE → binary addresses are fixed
Partial RELRO → GOT is still writable
Not stripped → function names are visible



File Analysis


                  
                  Explanation 
                  
                



File: vuln
Type: ELF 32-bit (LSB)
Architecture: Intel i386
Linking: Dynamically linked
System: GNU/Linux 3.2.0



Source code Analysis


                  
                  Explanation 
                  
                

From the source code, we can immediately see that this challenge is very similar to the previous one.

char buf[32];
However, unlike the previous challenge that used read(), this one uses:
fgets(buf, 128, stdin);
At first glance, fgets() looks safer because it allows the programmer to specify a maximum number of bytes to read. But the issue here is that the specified size is 128 bytes, while the actual buffer can only hold 32 bytes.
This means the program still accepts input far beyond the buffer’s capacity, resulting in a stack buffer overflow. So even though the input function changed from read() to fgets(), the bug remains essentially the same: the input limit is larger than the destination buffer.


win() Address


                  
                  Explanation 
                  
                

To get the target address for exploitation, we can use gdb and run info functions to list all available functions inside the binary.

From the output, we can clearly identify the win function:
0x08049276  win
This tells us that the address of win() is 0x08049276, which is the function we want to redirect execution to during the buffer overflow exploit.


Disas Vuln()


                  
                  Explanation 
                  
                

To determine the buffer offset, we can inspect the disassembly of vuln in gdb using disas vuln.

The important instruction here is:
lea eax, [ebp-0x28]
This shows that the input buffer starts at ebp-0x28, meaning the buffer offset is 0x28 bytes from the base pointer.
A bit more detail:

sub esp, 0x24 allocates local stack space
there is also saved padding/alignment such as the preserved ebx
as a result, the compiler places the buffer at ebp-0x28

So even though the declared buffer is:
char buf[32];
the actual stack offset to reach the saved frame area is 40 bytes (0x28), not just 32 bytes.
This is why we use 0x28 bytes of padding before overwriting the saved return address.


Solve.py


                  
                  Explanation 
                  
                

The payload uses 44 bytes before the target address because we need to fully reach the saved return address on the stack.




From the disassembly of vuln, we already identified that the buffer starts at:
lea eax, [ebp-0x28]
This means the distance from the start of the buffer to the base pointer is 0x28 bytes = 40 bytes.
After those 40 bytes, the next value on the stack is the saved EBP, which takes 4 bytes on a 32-bit binary.
So the total offset becomes:

40 bytes to fill the buffer up to saved EBP
+ 4 bytes to overwrite saved EBP
then the next 4 bytes land on the return address

That is why the payload uses:
b"A" * 44
In other words, 44 bytes are needed so the next value we write will overwrite EIP with the address of win().
On a 64-bit binary, this is often +8 instead of +4 because the saved base pointer (RBP) is 8 bytes wide. But since this challenge is 32-bit, the saved EBP is only 4 bytes.

Flag

PicoCTF-2026---Write-Up/Rev/4.-Bypassme.title: 4. Bypassme
PicoCTF-2026---Write-Up/Rev/4.-Bypassme.content:
File


Initial Observation

After a quick initial check ( strings -n 4 bypassme.bin ) , nothing immediately revealed the password
Running the binary shows this is clearly an authentication challenge
So the next step is to inspect the login logic with LLDB and recover / bypass the password check

LLDB



                  
                  Explanation 
                  
                


Switched to Intel syntax for easier reading
Set breakpoint at main and inspected disassembly
Found interesting call:

lea rax, [rbp-0x110]  
mov rdi, rax  
call decode_password  


This shows:
buffer at [rbp-0x110]
passed into decode_password()
so decoded password will be stored there






                  
                  Explanation 
                  
                


Set breakpoint right after decode_password (main + 55)
Run and inspect memory:

x/s $rbp - 0x110  



The buffer now contains the decoded password


Result:


SuperSecure  



Flag

PicoCTF-2026---Write-Up/Rev/5.-The-add-o.filePath: PicoCTF 2026 - Write Up/Rev/5. The add-on trap.md
PicoCTF-2026---Write-Up/Rev/5.-The-add-o.title: 5. The add-on trap
PicoCTF-2026---Write-Up/Rev/5.-The-add-o.content:
Initial Setup

Given file: .xpi add-on archive
Extracted it using password: picoctf


After extraction, contents were placed into: extracted_addon




Cd extracted_addon



                  
                  Explanation 
                  
                



After extracting, we inspect the contents of extracted_addon/


The most important file here is manifest.json


Why?

In browser extensions, manifest.json acts like an entry point
Similar to main() in C, but instead of code execution:

it defines the configuration and behavior of the extension





It usually contains:

which scripts run
what pages are loaded (e.g., popup.html)
permissions and resources



So our next step is to read manifest.json to understand how the extension works




Cat manifest.json



                  
                  Explanation 
                  
                



After inspecting manifest.json, most fields are just metadata:

name, version, icons → not important for logic



The interesting part is:
"background": {
  "scripts": ["background/main.js"]
}



This tells us:

background/main.js contains the main logic of the extension



So next step:

analyze background/main.js to understand what the extension actually does








                  
                  Explanation 
                  
                



The interesting values here are:

const webhookUrl = "..." → looks encrypted / encoded
const key = "..." → likely the key used to decrypt it



The comment is the big hint:
// TODO I must find a solution to remove the key from here, for now I'll leave it there because I need it to encrypt the webhook


That strongly suggests:

the key is temporarily hardcoded
and it is related to the protected webhookUrl



So the likely next step is:

use key to decrypt / recover the real webhook URL





Decryption


                  
                  Explanation 
                  
                



The ciphertext starts with gAAAA..., which is a strong indicator of Fernet encryption


Fernet is:

a symmetric encryption scheme from Python (cryptography library)
uses a URL-safe base64 key (32 bytes)
provides authenticated encryption (AES + HMAC)



This matches perfectly with:

the format of webhookUrl
the comment about a 32 url-safe base64-encoded key



So we can decrypt it using the provided key:
from cryptography.fernet import Fernet
 
key = b"PASTE_KEY_HERE"
ciphertext = b"PASTE_WEBHOOK_URL_HERE"
 
f = Fernet(key)
print(f.decrypt(ciphertext).decode())




Flag
picoCTF{Us3_4dd/0ns_v3ry_c4r3fully1}

PicoCTF-2026---Write-Up/Rev/6.-Silent-St.title: 6. Silent Stream
PicoCTF-2026---Write-Up/Rev/6.-Silent-St.content:
Given files:

encrypt.py
packets.pcap


Observation


packets.pcap is a packet capture file

it stores recorded network traffic
in this case, likely the captured file transfer



We also get encrypt.py

this shows how the file was processed before being sent
so it gives us the encoding/encryption logic used during transfer




Solver.py
from scapy.all import rdpcap, Raw
 
packets = rdpcap('packets.pcap')
sessions = packets.sessions()
 
for session_id, session_packets in sessions.items():
    encrypted_payload = b""
    for pkt in session_packets:
        if pkt.haslayer(Raw):
            encrypted_payload += pkt[Raw].load
 
    if not encrypted_payload:
        continue
 
    decrypted = bytearray((b - 42) % 256 for b in encrypted_payload)
 
    if decrypted.startswith(b'\xff\xd8\xff'):
        ext = '.jpg'
    elif decrypted.startswith(b'\x89PNG'):
        ext = '.png'
    else:
        try:
            decrypted.decode('utf-8')
            ext = '.txt'
        except:
            ext = '.bin'
 
    filename = f"extracted_{session_id.replace(':', '_')}{ext}"
    with open(filename, "wb") as f:
        f.write(decrypted)
    
 


                  
                  Explanation 
                  
                



After rebuilding each raw payload from packets.pcap, we decrypt it with:
(b - 42) % 256


This comes directly from encrypt.py, where each byte was encoded as:
(b + 42) % 256


So the reverse is simply:

encryption: +42 mod 256
decryption: -42 mod 256



Example:


4 + 2 mod 10 = 6


then 6 - 2 mod 10 = 4




At first, I tried treating the recovered data as text, but it came out as gibberish


That suggested the payload was probably not plain text


So the next intuition was to check common file signatures like JPEG or PNG


That is why the solver checks magic bytes

Magic bytes are the first bytes of a file that identify its format



In this script:

b'\xff\xd8\xff' → JPEG
b'\x89PNG' → PNG
otherwise:

if it decodes as UTF-8 → save as .txt
else → save as .bin





So the overall logic is:

rebuild payload from packets
undo the byte shift with -42
identify the file type from its magic bytes





Flag


                  
                  Explanation 
                  
                



After running the solver, the decrypted output was identified as a JPEG file


The script saved it as:
extracted_...jpg



Then simply open it:
xdg-open extracted_...jpg





The image contains the flag

PicoCTF-2026---Write-Up/Rev/7.-Auto-rev-.title: 7. Auto rev 1
PicoCTF-2026---Write-Up/Rev/7.-Auto-rev-.content:
Observation 
                  
                



At first, I thought the value shown at the top was some kind of seed


My initial idea was:

reverse the binary
use that seed to recover the secret



But that interpretation turned out to be wrong


The key clue is the wording:

“Here’s the next binary in bytes:”



That means the binary dump belongs to the next challenge step


So it would make no sense if the value printed before it was only a seed for reversing that same binary


After looking closer, the value printed before the dump is actually the secret itself


The hex binary is just there to mislead us into overthinking it


Conclusion

Read the leaked secret from the top line
Send it back
Repeat for all 20 rounds



Solve.py
from pwn import *
 
r = remote('mysterious-sea.picoctf.net', 63753)
 
r.recvuntil(b"Good luck >:)\n")
 
for i in range(20):
    print(f"\n=== Round {i+1}/20 ===")
 
    secret = r.recvline().strip()
    print(f"[*] Secret: {secret}")
 
    r.recvline()  # "Here's the next binary in bytes:"
 
    hex_data = r.recvuntil(b"What's the secret?:").strip()
 
    print(f"[*] Hex length: {len(hex_data)} chars = {len(hex_data)//2} bytes")
 
    r.sendline(secret)
 
    resp = r.recvline().strip()
    print(f"[*] Response: {resp}")
 
r.interactive()


                  
                  Explanation 
                  
                


After connecting, we wait until "Good luck" appears
Then for each round:

read the first line → this already contains the secret
skip the binary dump
wait until prompt "What's the secret?"
send the extracted secret with sendline()


Repeat this process 20 times to complete the challenge



Flag

PicoCTF-2026---Write-Up/Rev/8.-Secure-pa.filePath: PicoCTF 2026 - Write Up/Rev/8. Secure password DB.md
PicoCTF-2026---Write-Up/Rev/8.-Secure-pa.title: 8. Secure password DB

PicoCTF-2026---Write-Up/Rev/9.-Bin-ins-3.title: 9. Bin ins 3
PicoCTF-2026---Write-Up/Rev/9.-Bin-ins-3.content:
File

Quick check w/ strings

No meaningful strings found
Output mostly looks like gibberish

Ghidra - entry ( bin-ins.exe )



                  
                  Explanation 
                  
                



From the entry function, we don’t see any clear application logic


Instead, it mostly:

allocates memory
stores pointers / prepares buffers
performs a lot of setup-like operations



There is no obvious:

input handling
validation logic
or flag-related behavior



This makes the entry look more like a setup / loader stage, not the actual logic


So it raises suspicion that:

the real functionality might be stored elsewhere (e.g., another section)





Objdump -h



                  
                  Explanation 
                  
                


After checking the sections, one stands out:

.ATOM


Why it’s suspicious:

not a standard PE section name
relatively large in size (even bigger than .text)


This strengthens the earlier suspicion:

entry looked like a loader/setup stage
so this custom section likely contains the real payload/data


Next step:

extract .ATOM for further analysis





Extract .ATOM


                  
                  Explanation 
                  
                



Extract the .ATOM section using offset & size from objdump -h & Check the extracted file :



Decompress it & check again :


Result: Windows executable



Conclusion:

bin-ins.exe is just a loader/wrapper
real binary is hidden inside →  .ATOM





Quick check w/ strings

After extracting, we get a new executable (atom.out)
Usually, we do another quick check with strings
This helps to quickly spot:

hardcoded strings
possible flags
hints about program logic



From strings, we found:

Objdump -s -j .rdata atom.out | less


                  
                  Why using objdump -s -j .rdata 
                  
                



After getting an initial clue from strings, the next natural step is to inspect the section directly


The goal is no longer just “does this string exist?”, but:

where it appears
what surrounds it
whether nearby data gives more context



That is why I used:
objdump -s -j .rdata atom.out | less


Breakdown:

-s → dump section contents (hex + ASCII)
-j .rdata → focus on the section where strings are typically stored
| less → makes it searchable and easier to navigate





Flag


                  
                  Explanation 
                  
                




While inspecting .rdata using objdump -s -j .rdata atom.out | less, I searched for interesting patterns near the previous clue


Around that area, I found a suspicious base64-like string (highlighted in the dump)


The structure (mixed upper/lowercase + numbers + ending =) strongly suggests base64 encoding


So I tested it by decoding:



Conclusion:

The flag was embedded in .rdata, but encoded in base64
strings only gave a hint, but dumping the section revealed the full encoded data
Decoding it directly yields the flag
